mnemonics: Rice's Theorem: All Non-Trivial, Semantic Properties of Programs are Undecidable (Think: 'No Program can Decide its Own Semantic Fate')
keyPoints: A property is trivial if either every recursive language has it, or no recursive language has it.
Rice's theorem only applies to semantic properties (i.e., properties about the function the language computes), not syntactic ones (like whether the program has a loop).
This theorem is a powerful tool for proving undecidability; if a problem is a non-trivial semantic property, you can immediately conclude it is undecidable.
commonMistakes: Applying Rice's theorem to syntactic properties (e.g., 'does this TM have more than 5 states?') – these may be decidable.
Assuming Rice's theorem says a problem is unrecognizable (it only proves undecidability; some undecidable problems are still recognizable).
Forgetting that emptiness (L(M) = ∅) is a non-trivial semantic property and is therefore undecidable.
